fact: The tornado touched down in Missouri and traveled across three states (Missouri, Illinois, Indiana) without lifting, lasting about three and a half hours on March 18, 1925.
source: The Tri-State Tornado: The Story of America's Greatest Tornado Disaster, Peter S. Felknor (1992); also NOAA Storm Prediction Center

fact: The U.S. Weather Bureau had banned the word 'tornado' from its forecasts out of fear it would cause panic.
source: Storm Watchers: The Turbulent History of Weather Prediction, John D. Cox (2002); also widely documented in NOAA historical accounts

fact: The tornado's forward speed reached approximately 60 miles per hour, faster than most automobiles of the era.
source: NOAA/National Weather Service, Tri-State Tornado historical summary

fact: 695 people were killed by the Tri-State Tornado, making it the deadliest single tornado in U.S. history.
source: NOAA Storm Prediction Center; also Significant Tornadoes 1680–1991, Thomas P. Grazulis (1993)

fact: The tornado's continuous path stretched 219 miles, the longest tornado track on record in the United States.
source: NOAA National Weather Service; note: some modern reanalysis suggests it may have been a tornado family, but 219 miles remains the official record.
